---
title: "Chapter 45 — 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 44 · 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability](ch44.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [46 · ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance →](ch46.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Most machine learning is a sophisticated pattern-matcher: it learns which inputs tend to go with which outputs. But "tends to go with" is correlation, and decisions need cause. If you want to know what *happens when you intervene* — raise a price, prescribe a drug, change a ranking — you need causal inference: the study of how to estimate the effect of an action from data. This chapter sits at the responsibility-and-frontier edge of the field because causal thinking is what makes ML robust, fair, and actionable rather than merely accurate.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Production, Responsibility & Frontier · used to estimate the effect of interventions and answer "what if" questions · the one key idea is that prediction tells you what to *expect*, but only causal reasoning tells you what to *do*.
:::

## 45.1 — Why correlation is not causation

Every ML practitioner has heard the slogan, but the mechanism behind it is worth making precise. A predictive model learns $P(Y \mid X)$ — the distribution of the outcome given what it observes. That is exactly the right object when the world keeps generating data the same way and you only want to *forecast*. It is the wrong object the moment you *act*, because acting changes the data-generating process itself.

The classic culprit is a **confounder**: a variable that influences both the treatment and the outcome, creating a statistical association that is not a causal path. Ice-cream sales correlate with drowning deaths — not because cones are deadly, but because hot weather drives both. Weather is the confounder. A model that "predicts drownings from ice-cream sales" will score well on held-out data and be completely useless as a basis for banning ice cream.

The trouble is that prediction accuracy gives you no warning. A confounded feature is a *genuinely* predictive feature; it lowers your loss. The model has no way to distinguish a causal signal from a spurious one, because both reduce error identically on data drawn from the same distribution.

```{mermaid}
graph TD
    W[Hot weather] --> I[Ice-cream sales]
    W --> D[Drownings]
    I -.spurious correlation.-> D
```

This is why causal questions need extra structure that the data alone cannot supply — assumptions about *which arrows exist*. No amount of clever fitting recovers a cause from a correlation without them.

::: {.callout-warning}
"The model has 0.95 AUC, so these features must be driving the outcome" is a category error. High predictive accuracy is fully compatible with every important feature being a confounded proxy. Accuracy validates prediction, never causation.
:::

## 45.2 — Simpson's paradox: when aggregates lie

Simpson's paradox is the sharpest illustration that a correlation can not only be spurious but can *reverse sign* once you account for a confounder. A treatment can look harmful in the pooled data and helpful in every subgroup — simultaneously.

Consider a kidney-stone study comparing two treatments. Treatment A looks worse overall, yet beats Treatment B on both small stones and large stones:

| Group | Treatment A | Treatment B |
|---|---|---|
| Small stones | 81/87 = 93% | 234/270 = 87% |
| Large stones | 192/263 = 73% | 55/80 = 69% |
| **Overall** | **273/350 = 78%** | **289/350 = 83%** |

A wins both rows but loses the total. The trick is that stone size is a confounder: doctors gave the tougher Treatment A to the harder (large-stone) cases. The aggregate mixes "which treatment" with "how sick the patient was."

The paradox dissolves once you ask the *causal* question — what would happen if we assigned treatment regardless of stone size — instead of the observational one. The lesson generalizes far beyond medicine: any time you average over a group whose composition differs across the thing you are comparing, the headline number can point the wrong way. Whether to condition on a variable or not is not a statistical choice you can make from the table; it requires knowing the causal roles of the variables (see §45.5).

## 45.3 — Potential outcomes and counterfactuals

To reason carefully about effects, we need a language for things that *did not happen*. The **potential outcomes** framework (also called the Neyman–Rubin model) gives each unit two outcomes: $Y_i(1)$, what would happen if unit $i$ were treated, and $Y_i(0)$, what would happen if it were not. The causal effect for that unit is the difference:

$$\tau_i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)$$

Here is the catch, often called the **fundamental problem of causal inference**: for any given unit you only ever observe *one* of the two. A patient who took the drug reveals $Y_i(1)$; their $Y_i(0)$ — what would have happened without it — is a **counterfactual** you can never measure. Individual effects are therefore unidentifiable without assumptions.

What we *can* hope to estimate are averages. The **Average Treatment Effect** is

$$\text{ATE} = \mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0)]$$

and the **Conditional Average Treatment Effect** narrows it to a subgroup with features $x$:

$$\text{CATE}(x) = \mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid X = x]$$

The naive estimate — average outcome among the treated minus average among the untreated — equals the ATE only when the two groups are *exchangeable*: comparable in everything that affects the outcome. The bias of the naive comparison is exactly the confounding:

$$\underbrace{\mathbb{E}[Y \mid T{=}1] - \mathbb{E}[Y \mid T{=}0]}_{\text{observed}} = \text{ATE} + \underbrace{\big(\mathbb{E}[Y(0)\mid T{=}1] - \mathbb{E}[Y(0)\mid T{=}0]\big)}_{\text{selection bias}}$$

The second term is the difference in baseline outcomes between groups that would exist *even with no treatment*. Make it zero and the observed gap is the causal effect. Everything in causal inference is, at bottom, a strategy for zeroing that term.

The assumptions that license the move from observed gap to causal effect have standard names. **Ignorability** (also "unconfoundedness" or "conditional exchangeability") says that, once you condition on the right covariates $X$, treatment assignment is independent of the potential outcomes: $\{Y(0), Y(1)\} \perp T \mid X$. **Positivity** (or "overlap") says every unit has a real chance of either treatment, $0 < P(T=1 \mid X=x) < 1$, so there is something to compare. And **SUTVA** (the stable-unit-treatment-value assumption) says one unit's treatment does not spill over onto another's outcome, and there is only one version of the treatment. These three are the contract you sign whenever you estimate a causal effect from observational data; break any one and the estimate stops being causal.

::: {.callout-tip}
A clean mental check: a causal claim is a claim about a counterfactual. If you cannot phrase it as "the same unit, had things been different," you are probably describing a correlation, not an effect.
:::

## 45.4 — Randomized experiments: the gold standard

There is one move that zeroes the selection-bias term by construction: **randomization**. If you assign treatment by a coin flip, then treatment is statistically independent of everything about the unit — including the counterfactual $Y(0)$. The treated and untreated groups are, in expectation, identical in every baseline characteristic, measured or not. That last word is what makes randomization uniquely powerful: it balances confounders you never thought to record. In the language of §45.3, randomization makes ignorability hold *by design* rather than by assumption, and you no longer need to have measured the confounders at all.

This is the logic of the **randomized controlled trial** (RCT) and its industrial cousin, the **A/B test**. Split users randomly into control (A) and treatment (B), ship the change to B only, and compare. Under randomization the difference in means is an unbiased estimate of the ATE:

$$\widehat{\text{ATE}} = \bar{Y}_{\text{treated}} - \bar{Y}_{\text{control}}$$

```python
import numpy as np
# simulate an A/B test: true effect of +0.20 on conversion
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 10_000
T = rng.integers(0, 2, n)            # random assignment -> no confounding
base = rng.normal(0, 1, n)           # hidden user quality (a confounder if it leaked into T)
Y = 0.5 + 0.20 * T + 0.3 * base + rng.normal(0, 1, n)
ate = Y[T == 1].mean() - Y[T == 0].mean()   # difference in means
print(round(ate, 3))                  # ~0.20: recovers the truth because T is random
```

Randomization is the gold standard, but it is not free. Experiments can be unethical (you cannot randomize people into smoking), impossible (you cannot rerun history), slow, expensive, or underpowered for rare outcomes. They also estimate the effect *for the population and setting you tested*, which may not transfer. And they go wrong in practice through broken randomization, **interference** (one unit's treatment affects another — the SUTVA violation that haunts social networks and marketplaces), and peeking at results before the sample is collected. When a real experiment is off the table, we fall back on observational methods — the subject of the next two sections.

## 45.5 — Causal graphs, the do-operator, and identification

Judea Pearl's contribution was to give causal assumptions a *diagram* and a *calculus*. A **causal DAG** (directed acyclic graph) draws each variable as a node and each direct causal influence as an arrow. The graph encodes which correlations are causal and which are not, by exposing the *paths* along which association flows.

Three building blocks cover every graph:

- **Chain** $A \to B \to C$: $B$ is a mediator; $A$ and $C$ are associated, and conditioning on $B$ blocks it.
- **Fork** $A \leftarrow B \to C$: $B$ is a common cause (confounder); $A$ and $C$ are associated spuriously, and conditioning on $B$ blocks it.
- **Collider** $A \to B \leftarrow C$: $A$ and $C$ are *not* associated — until you condition on $B$, which *creates* a spurious association.

The rule that unifies all three is **d-separation**: a path is blocked if it contains a chain or fork whose middle node you condition on, *or* a collider whose middle node (and all its descendants) you do *not* condition on. Two variables are independent whenever every path between them is blocked. That single criterion is the engine that reads conditional independencies straight off the graph.

That collider rule is the counterintuitive one and the source of much bad analysis: controlling for the wrong variable can manufacture confounding rather than remove it (see §45.7).

To express intervention, Pearl introduces the **do-operator**. $P(Y \mid X = x)$ is *seeing* — the distribution of $Y$ among units that happen to have $X = x$. $P(Y \mid \text{do}(X = x))$ is *doing* — the distribution if we reach in and *set* $X = x$ for everyone. Setting $X$ deletes every arrow *into* $X$ (we overrode its natural causes), which is precisely the graph surgery a randomized experiment performs physically.

```{mermaid}
graph LR
    subgraph Observational
      Z1[Z] --> X1[X]
      Z1 --> Y1[Y]
      X1 --> Y1
    end
    subgraph do_X["do(X=x): cut arrows into X"]
      Z2[Z] --> Y2[Y]
      X2[X = x] --> Y2
    end
```

The goal of **identification** is to rewrite a do-expression in terms of ordinary (observational) probabilities, so it can be estimated from data we already have. Two criteria do most of the work:

- **Backdoor criterion:** a set $Z$ of variables suffices to estimate $X \to Y$ if it blocks every "backdoor" path (every non-causal path from $X$ to $Y$ that starts with an arrow into $X$) and contains no descendants of $X$. Then $$P(Y \mid \text{do}(x)) = \sum_z P(Y \mid x, z)\, P(z).$$ This is "adjust for the confounders" made rigorous — and it tells you *exactly which* variables to adjust for, no more (avoiding colliders) and no less.
- **Frontdoor criterion:** when a confounder is *unmeasured*, you can sometimes still identify the effect by routing it through a fully observed mediator $M$ on the path $X \to M \to Y$, provided nothing confounds $M \to Y$. It rescues cases the backdoor cannot touch.

::: {.callout-tip}
Draw the DAG *before* you choose your regression controls. The graph, not the software, decides which variables belong in the model. "Throw everything in and let the model sort it out" is how colliders sneak in and bias your estimate.
:::

## 45.6 — Observational estimators

When you have a DAG and a valid adjustment set, you still need a *method* to compute the effect. Each of the workhorse observational estimators is a different trick for making the treated and untreated comparable.

**Matching.** Pair each treated unit with an untreated unit that has near-identical covariates, then compare within pairs. Intuitively you are reconstructing the missing counterfactual from a real twin. It is transparent but struggles in high dimensions, where exact twins do not exist.

**Propensity scores.** Rosenbaum and Rubin's insight: instead of matching on many covariates, match on a single summary — the **propensity score** $e(x) = P(T = 1 \mid X = x)$, the probability of being treated given covariates. Units with the same propensity are balanced on $X$ on average. You can match on it, or use **inverse-propensity weighting (IPW)**, which reweights each unit by $1/e(x)$ for treated and $1/(1-e(x))$ for controls to build a pseudo-population where treatment is independent of $X$:

$$\widehat{\text{ATE}}_{\text{IPW}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i \left( \frac{T_i Y_i}{e(X_i)} - \frac{(1-T_i) Y_i}{1 - e(X_i)} \right)$$

The danger: propensities near 0 or 1 produce enormous weights and wild variance — a sign of **positivity** violation (some units essentially never get one treatment).

**Instrumental variables (IV).** When an unmeasured confounder defeats the backdoor, an **instrument** $Z$ can save you. An instrument affects the treatment but touches the outcome *only* through the treatment, and shares no confounder with it ($Z \to X \to Y$, with no $Z \to Y$ shortcut and no back door). Random nudges, lottery numbers, and policy quirks make good instruments. IV exploits only the variation in $X$ that the instrument induces, which is "as good as random." The classic estimator, for a binary instrument, is the **Wald ratio** — the effect of $Z$ on $Y$ divided by the effect of $Z$ on $X$:

$$\widehat{\tau}_{\text{IV}} = \frac{\mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z{=}1] - \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z{=}0]}{\mathbb{E}[X \mid Z{=}1] - \mathbb{E}[X \mid Z{=}0]}$$

Intuitively, the instrument nudges some units into treatment; you measure how much their outcomes moved and divide out how much the nudge actually shifted treatment. A weak instrument — a tiny denominator — blows this ratio up, which is why instrument *strength* matters as much as validity.

**Difference-in-differences (DiD).** When a treatment hits one group at a known time, compare the *change* in the treated group to the *change* in an untreated control group over the same period. Subtracting each group's own baseline cancels fixed differences between them; subtracting the control's trend cancels whatever was happening anyway. Its load-bearing assumption is **parallel trends**: absent treatment, the two groups would have moved in parallel.

**Regression discontinuity (RDD).** When treatment is assigned by a sharp cutoff on some running variable — a test score above 60 gets the scholarship — units just above and just below the threshold are essentially identical except for treatment. Comparing outcomes in a narrow window around the cutoff yields a near-experimental local estimate.

| Method | Defeats unmeasured confounding? | Key assumption | Typical use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Matching / propensity | No — needs all confounders measured | Ignorability + positivity | Observational cohorts |
| Instrumental variables | Yes | Valid, relevant, exclusion-restricted instrument | Econometrics, Mendelian randomization |
| Difference-in-differences | Yes (time-invariant ones) | Parallel trends | Policy roll-outs |
| Regression discontinuity | Yes (locally) | Continuity at the cutoff | Threshold-based programs |

::: {.callout-warning}
Propensity-score and matching methods only remove confounding from the variables you measured. They are *not* a substitute for randomization — an unmeasured confounder passes straight through them, leaving a biased estimate that looks perfectly precise.
:::

## 45.7 — Heterogeneous effects and uplift modeling in ML

Averages hide the people who matter most. The ATE might be a modest +2% lift, but that can mask a population where the treatment helps some users a lot, does nothing for most, and *backfires* for a few. For decisions — who to send the coupon to, who to give the drug — you want the **CATE**, $\tau(x) = \mathbb{E}[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid X=x]$: the effect *conditional on* who the unit is. Estimating that function is where modern ML and causal inference meet.

In marketing this is called **uplift modeling**, and it reframes the target. A naive churn model predicts *who will leave*; an uplift model predicts *whom an intervention will change*. Customers fall into four types, and only one is worth treating:

- **Persuadables** — buy only if treated. The whole point.
- **Sure things** — buy regardless. Treating them wastes budget.
- **Lost causes** — never buy. Treating them wastes budget.
- **Sleeping dogs** — *stop* buying if treated. Treating them is actively harmful.

A plain response model happily targets sure things (high predicted purchase). Uplift targets the *difference* the treatment makes, and learns to leave sleeping dogs alone.

The catch is that the training label — the individual uplift — is never observed (the fundamental problem again), so we need estimators that infer it. The simplest is the **T-learner**: fit one model on the treated, one on the control, and subtract.

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor as GBR

# T-learner: two outcome models, difference = CATE estimate
def t_learner(X, T, Y):
    m1 = GBR().fit(X[T == 1], Y[T == 1])   # learns E[Y|X, treated]
    m0 = GBR().fit(X[T == 0], Y[T == 0])   # learns E[Y|X, control]
    return lambda Xnew: m1.predict(Xnew) - m0.predict(Xnew)  # tau_hat(x)
```

Richer variants address the T-learner's weaknesses: the **S-learner** uses a single model with treatment as a feature; the **X-learner** cross-fits to handle imbalanced groups; **causal forests** adapt random forests to estimate $\tau(x)$ with valid confidence intervals; and **doubly robust** learners (DR-learner, double machine learning) combine an outcome model and a propensity model so that getting *either* one right yields a consistent estimate — a useful insurance policy. All of these assume no unmeasured confounding; ML makes the *estimation* flexible, but it cannot manufacture the *identification* that only the experiment or the DAG can provide.

::: {.callout-warning}
Plugging a powerful gradient-boosted or deep model into observational data does not make its predictions causal. A flexible learner fits confounded associations beautifully. Identification is an assumption about the world, not a property of the model class — no architecture upgrades it for you.
:::

## 45.8 — Why causal thinking matters for AI

Causal reasoning is not an academic luxury bolted onto ML; it is what lets a model survive contact with the real world.

**Robustness and distribution shift.** Causal relationships are stable across environments in a way that correlations are not. A model that leans on a spurious feature (cows usually appear on grass, so "grass" predicts "cow") shatters when the distribution moves (a cow on a beach). Learning features tied to the *causal* mechanism is one of the most promising routes to out-of-distribution generalization.

**Fairness.** Many fairness questions are inherently counterfactual: *would this decision have been the same had the applicant been of a different group, all else equal?* Causal graphs let you separate a legitimate pathway from a discriminatory one and decide which to allow — a distinction that purely statistical parity metrics cannot make.

**Actionability and explanation.** A recommendation is only useful if acting on it produces the predicted change. "Raise this user's engagement score" is worthless if the score is an effect, not a cause. Counterfactual explanations — "the loan would have been approved had income been \$5k higher" — are causal statements, and agents that plan and use tools (Chapter 33) are implicitly reasoning about the effects of their actions.

The throughline of this chapter: prediction answers *what will happen if the world keeps running as it has*; causal inference answers *what will happen if I change it*. As AI moves from forecasting to deciding and acting, the second question becomes the one that matters.

## 45.x — Key takeaways

- Predictive models learn $P(Y\mid X)$ (seeing); decisions need $P(Y\mid \text{do}(X))$ (doing). High accuracy never certifies a causal claim.
- **Confounding** creates spurious association; Simpson's paradox shows it can even reverse the sign of an effect once you condition correctly.
- The **potential outcomes** framework defines effects via counterfactuals $Y(1), Y(0)$; you only ever observe one per unit (the fundamental problem), so we estimate averages (ATE, CATE).
- Observational causal claims rest on three assumptions: **ignorability**, **positivity**, and **SUTVA** — break any one and the estimate stops being causal.
- **Randomization** (RCTs, A/B tests) is the gold standard because it makes ignorability hold by design, balancing measured *and* unmeasured confounders.
- **Causal DAGs** + the **do-operator** make assumptions explicit; **d-separation** plus the **backdoor** and **frontdoor** criteria tell you exactly which variables to adjust for — and which to avoid (colliders).
- Observational estimators — matching/propensity, IV, difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity — each trade a different assumption for comparability; only IV/DiD/RDD can beat *unmeasured* confounding.
- **Uplift / CATE models** (T/S/X-learners, causal forests, double ML) bring ML flexibility to heterogeneous effects, but identification is an assumption, not something a bigger model supplies.
- Causal thinking underpins robustness to distribution shift, counterfactual fairness, and actionable, explainable AI.

## 45.y — See also

- **Chapter 03 — The Machine Learning Process** — experimental design, validation, and where causal questions enter a project.
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection** — A/B testing mechanics and the statistics of comparing groups.
- **Chapter 12 — Bayesian Machine Learning** — priors and probabilistic reasoning that underlie many causal estimators.
- **Chapter 13 — Probabilistic Graphical Models** — the DAG machinery and d-separation that causal graphs build on.
- **Chapter 44 — Explainable AI & Interpretability** — counterfactual explanations and feature attributions as causal statements.
- **Chapter 46 — AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance** — counterfactual fairness and the policy stakes of acting on causal claims.
- **Chapter 33 — AI Agents & Tool Use** — agents reasoning about the effects of their own actions.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 44 · 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability](ch44.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [46 · ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance →](ch46.html)
